title: Workflow Templates
description: Design and build workflow templates using activities, connectors, robots, terminal nodes, and links. Compare workflow design approaches and simplify complex processes with block and proxy processes.

topics.title: Workflow Template Concepts

content.text: Workflow templates are a core component of advanced life cycles in Windchill. They establish the specific events and tasks that must occur within each life cycle state, enabling organizations to enforce company-specific and industry-specific business processes consistently.
Workflow Template Fundamentals

Each workflow template manages a specific object type and is applied to that object type's life cycle. A workflow defines the sequence of actions, reviews, and approvals that must happen when an object enters a given state. You access workflow templates through Utilities > Workflow Template Administration.

rows: Activity Node
Assigns tasks to users for review, approval, or data entry
Review Document activity assigned to a Product Manager
Connector Node
Controls process flow logic (And, Or, Conditional, Threshold)
Or connector that routes based on first incoming link
Robot Node
Automates server-side tasks without user interaction
Set State robot that changes an object's life cycle state
Terminal Node
Ends a process branch or the entire workflow
End node that stops all active activities and completes the workflow
Link
Connects nodes and defines flow control (events and actions)
Link from an activity to a robot triggered by a Release event

content.text: Creating a Workflow Template

content.prompt: Arrange the steps for creating a workflow template in the correct order:
items: Define workflow properties (name, category, responsible role)
Add process nodes (activities, connectors, robots, terminals)
Link nodes together to define process flow
Define individual node properties (participants, routing, deadlines)
Validate, save, and check in the template
Test by initiating a workflow instance

content.text: The Responsible Role on a workflow template serves as the backup role for role resolution issues and receives deadline and error notifications. If no responsible role is set, these notifications default to the process initiator.

keyTakeaways: Workflow templates define the events and tasks required within advanced life cycle states
Workflows are composed of five building blocks: activity nodes, connector nodes, robot nodes, terminal nodes, and links
Each workflow template manages a specific object type and is applied to its life cycle
The Responsible Role acts as a backup for role resolution and receives error/deadline notifications

topics.title: Workflow Design Approaches

content.text: Choosing the right workflow design approach is critical because it determines how life cycle state transitions are managed, how much server load is generated, and how maintainable your configuration will be over time. Windchill supports four distinct approaches for managing life cycle evolution.
Four Approaches to Life Cycle Evolution

cards.back: Reuses workflow templates across multiple phases and gates. The same workflow is used in multiple states, reducing complexity. This approach is required when gates are used. Example: a Submit workflow used in 3 phases and a Review workflow used in 2 gates.

One large workflow placed in the first state's phase handles all state transitions. Provides easier one-to-one life cycle/workflow mapping and better flexibility on event configuration. Used for change management objects and promotion requests. However, generates extra server load and is not suitable for large-quantity objects or long-running processes.

Mixes workflows with manual Set State actions. Reduces workflow complexity by only automating state transitions that require formal processes. Example: In Work to Design uses manual Set State, while Design to Released uses a formal workflow.

Object state is driven by another business object's workflow (e.g., a change notice). No workflow is needed on the object itself, and no advanced life cycle is required. Handles release of multiple objects in a single procedure and limits the number of running workflows.

content.text: Comparing Approaches

rows: Modular
Reusable, gate-based life cycles
Requires careful template design for reuse
Single
Change management objects, promotion requests
Generates extra server load; not for high-volume objects
Combined
Partially formalized processes
Manual Set State steps lack audit trail enforcement
Change/Promotion Request
Releasing multiple objects together
State changes depend on external business object workflow

content.text: The Single approach generates extra server load because the workflow runs continuously from the first state to the last. Avoid it for object types that exist in high quantities or require long-running processes.

content.prompt: Match each scenario to the best workflow design approach:

pairs.left: Same review process used across multiple life cycle phases
A change notice drives state changes for multiple parts
Only some state transitions need formal workflow control
An ECN requires one continuous workflow from creation to completion

keyTakeaways: Four workflow design approaches: Modular, Single, Combined, and Change/Promotion Request
The Modular approach is required when gates are used and promotes template reuse across phases
The Single approach suits change management objects but generates extra server load
The Change/Promotion Request approach drives object states from an external workflow, limiting running workflows

topics.title: Workflow Nodes: Activities and Connectors

content.text: Activity and connector nodes form the interactive and logical backbone of any workflow. Activities assign tasks to users, while connectors control how the process branches, converges, and routes based on events and conditions.
Activity Nodes

An Assigned Activity is a user task such as reviewing, approving, completing a worksheet, or voting. When a workflow reaches an assigned activity, the designated users receive the task in their Tasks table. The task form includes instructions, a link to the primary business object, input fields, and a Complete Task button. An Ad Hoc Activity is a special activity where the recipient can create and assign additional activities at runtime. Activity properties are configured through tabs: General (name/category), Activity (form, instructions, digital signatures), Deadline, Participants (who receives the task), Routing (exit conditions like Approve/Reject), and Execution Options (error handling).
Connector Node Types

cards.back: The entry point of every workflow. Each process has exactly one Start connector. It cannot be removed or duplicated.
Waits for all incoming links to fire before continuing. Use for synchronization points where multiple parallel branches must complete before proceeding.
Continues when any incoming link fires. Terminates any incomplete predecessor activities on other branches. Use for exclusive routing where the first completed path wins.
Continues based on custom Java criteria. Evaluates a programmatic condition to determine the next path. Requires development knowledge.
Continues after a set number of incoming links fire. Useful for majority-vote scenarios where you need, for example, 3 out of 5 approvals to proceed.

content.text: The Or connector not only continues when any incoming link fires but also terminates incomplete predecessor activities on the other branches. This is critical in exclusive routing scenarios where once one reviewer approves, other reviewers' tasks should be removed.

content.prompt: Match each connector type to its behavior:

pairs.right: Waits for all incoming links before continuing
Continues when any incoming link fires
Continues after a set number of incoming links fire
Evaluates custom Java criteria to determine the path

keyTakeaways: Assigned Activities create user tasks for review, approval, or data entry; Ad Hoc Activities allow runtime task creation
Activity properties include participants, routing events, deadlines, and digital signature requirements
Five connector types control flow: Start, And, Or, Conditional, and Threshold
The Or connector terminates incomplete predecessor activities when any incoming link fires

topics.title: Robots and Terminal Nodes

content.text: Robot nodes automate tasks that would otherwise require manual user action, reducing human effort and enforcing consistency. Terminal nodes control how workflow branches and processes end. Together, they complete the set of building blocks needed to design fully automated workflows.
Basic Robot Types

rows: Notification
Sends email to users in a recipient list
Notify stakeholders when a document reaches a review state
Subscription Notification
Sends email to users who have subscribed to the object
Alert subscribers when an object changes state
Checkpoint/Method
Executes standard Windchill functions on the object
Set life cycle state, check in an object, or update attributes
Timer
Introduces a time delay in the process
Wait 24 hours before sending a reminder notification

content.text: Advanced Robot Types

cards.back: Launches a server-side application. Requires knowledge of the server environment and application APIs. Used for integrations with external systems.
Executes a Java expression within the workflow context. Enables custom logic such as conditional attribute updates or complex calculations.
Synchronizes the workflow to a Windchill event or Java expression. Pauses the process until the specified event occurs, enabling event-driven workflows.
Communicates with another server via URL. Used for cross-system integration such as triggering actions in an external ERP or PLM system.

content.text: Advanced robots (Application, Expression, Synchronization, URL) require Java, Info*Engine, or API knowledge to configure. Involve your development team when configuring these robot types. Every workflow also needs terminal nodes: the End node stops the entire process (all active activities terminate), while the Ground node ends only the current branch, allowing parallel branches to continue.

content.prompt: Arrange these robot types from simplest (no coding required) to most complex (requires Java/API knowledge):

keyTakeaways: Basic robots (Notification, Subscription Notification, Checkpoint/Method, Timer) require no coding and handle common automation tasks
Advanced robots (Application, Expression, Synchronization, URL) require Java or API knowledge for cross-system integrations
The End terminal node stops the entire workflow; the Ground node ends only the current branch
Every workflow must have at least one path leading to an End node

topics.title: Links and Process Simplification

content.text: Links are the connections between workflow nodes that define flow control — which actions occur when predecessor nodes complete. Understanding link properties, loop links, and process simplification techniques is essential for building maintainable and effective workflows.
Link Properties: Events and Actions

Every link has a source node, a target node, an event, and an action. Read a link as: "When [source] sends [event], do [action] to [target]." The most common action types are Start (starts the target activity) and Fire (sends an event to a connector, which may or may not start depending on its logic). Common events include Complete (the default when no routing is defined) and routing events like Release or Rework, which replace Complete when routing is configured.

When an activity has routing configured (e.g., Release/Rework), the Complete event is replaced by the routing events. Each routing event can trigger a different link, sending the process down a different path. A Loop Link is a special link type that re-enables nodes that have already completed — essential for rework cycles, since finished nodes disable themselves and ordinary links cannot restart them.
Block and Proxy Processes

rows: Block Process
A sub-process built inside a parent workflow
Stored within the parent workflow; can only be referenced by the parent. Used to organize complex sections visually.
Proxy Process
A reference to a standalone workflow template
Built and managed independently by the Workflow Administrator. Reusable across multiple parent workflows.

content.text: Use Proxy Processes when you want to reuse a sub-process across multiple parent workflows. Use Block Processes when the sub-process is unique to a single parent and you want to keep everything contained.

content.prompt: Match each link or process concept to its description:

pairs.right: Begins execution of the target activity node
Sends an event to a connector node
Re-enables previously completed nodes for rework cycles
Standalone sub-process reusable across multiple workflows

keyTakeaways: Links connect nodes using an event-action model: when the source sends an event, the action executes on the target
Loop Links re-enable completed nodes, which is essential for rework cycles since ordinary links cannot restart disabled nodes
Block Processes are sub-processes contained within a parent workflow; Proxy Processes are standalone and reusable
Routing events (Release, Rework) replace the default Complete event when routing is configured on an activity

topics.title: Exercise: Edit Workflow Templates

content.text: In this exercise, you will edit an existing workflow template to add a dedicated review process. You will modify activity properties, configure routing events, and create links with loop behavior for rework cycles.
You must have access to the PTC Publications context and the Workflow Template Administration utility to complete this exercise.

content.title: Edit Workflow Templates
content.objective: Modify the Plan Development Workflow by editing an activity, configuring routing, and creating links with loop behavior.

steps.action: Navigate to PTC Publications > Utilities > Workflow Template Administration.
steps.detail: The Workflow Template Administration utility lists all workflow templates available in the current context. You can create, edit, and manage workflow templates from here.
steps.hint: Use the site navigator or context menu to access Utilities.

steps.action: Right-click the Plan Development Workflow and select Edit.
steps.detail: Opening the workflow for editing launches the graphical Workflow Template Editor. This editor displays the process flow as a visual diagram with nodes and links.
steps.hint: The workflow must be checked out before editing. If prompted, check out the workflow first.

steps.action: Review the existing workflow properties, nodes, and tasks to understand the current process flow.
steps.detail: Before making changes, familiarize yourself with the existing structure. Note which activities, robots, and connectors are already in place and how they are connected.

steps.action: Click Activity 2 in the workflow editor and rename it to 'Review Document'.
steps.detail: Renaming the activity clarifies its purpose in the process flow. Activity names appear in users' task lists, so a descriptive name helps users understand what they need to do.
steps.hint: Double-click the activity or right-click and select Properties to edit the name.

steps.action: Set the Responsible Role to Product Manager.
steps.detail: The responsible role determines who receives the task and who is notified of deadlines. Setting it to Product Manager ensures the review is assigned to the correct team member.

steps.action: In the Activity tab, set the instructions to guide the reviewer to choose Release or Rework.
steps.detail: Clear instructions help reviewers understand what is expected. Specify the criteria for choosing Release (approved) versus Rework (needs changes).
steps.hint: Activity instructions appear on the task form when the user opens the task.

steps.action: In the Participants tab, remove the Creator role and add the Product Manager role.
steps.detail: Participants define who receives the activity task. Removing Creator and adding Product Manager ensures the review goes to the appropriate role rather than the document creator.

steps.action: In the Routing tab, set Manual Exclusive routing type with Release and Rework events.
steps.detail: Manual Exclusive routing requires the user to choose one routing event (Release or Rework) when completing the task. This replaces the default Complete event with the two routing options.
steps.hint: Select 'Manual Exclusive' from the routing type dropdown, then add Release and Rework as routing events.

steps.action: Review the Set State method robot properties, noting the Robot Type and Specific State settings.
steps.detail: The Set State robot automatically changes the object's life cycle state when triggered. Verify that it is configured to transition the document to the correct target state upon release.

tasks.title: Create and Configure Review Document Routing

steps.action: Place an Or connector below the Submit Document activity.
steps.detail: The Or connector will serve as a junction point for the rework loop. When a Rework event fires, the process routes through this connector back to the Submit Document activity.
steps.hint: Drag an Or connector from the node palette onto the workflow canvas.

steps.action: Create a link from the Review Document activity to the Or connector.
steps.detail: This link carries the Rework event from the review activity to the Or connector, which will then route back to the submission step.

steps.action: Create a link from the Or connector to the Submit Document activity.
steps.detail: This link completes the rework loop, sending the process back to Submit Document so the author can revise and resubmit the document.

steps.action: Configure the link from Review Document to the Set State robot: set the event to Release and the action to Start.
steps.detail: This link means: when the reviewer chooses Release, start the Set State robot to transition the document to the Released state.
steps.hint: Select the link and set Event = Release, Action = Start.

steps.action: Configure the link from Review Document to the Or connector: set the event to Rework, the action to Fire, and enable the Loop Link checkbox.
steps.detail: The Loop Link checkbox is critical here. Without it, the Submit Document activity (which has already completed) cannot be re-enabled. The loop link re-activates previously completed nodes.
steps.hint: Select the link, set Event = Rework, Action = Fire, and check the Loop Link checkbox.

steps.action: Select File > Save to save the workflow template.
steps.detail: Saving preserves your changes to the workflow template. The template is still checked out at this point.

steps.action: Close the Workflow Template Editor and check in the workflow template.
steps.detail: Checking in the workflow makes it available for use. Until checked in, other users cannot see your changes and the workflow cannot be used by life cycle transitions.
steps.hint: Right-click the workflow in the administration list and select Check In.

keyTakeaways: Activity routing replaces the default Complete event with specific routing events like Release and Rework
Loop Links must be enabled when routing back to previously completed nodes in rework cycles
The Or connector serves as a junction point for merging process paths
Workflow templates must be checked in before they can be used by life cycle transitions

topics.title: Exercise: Use Advanced Life Cycle Templates

content.text: In this exercise, you will convert a basic life cycle to advanced, connect it to the workflow template you edited in the previous exercise, configure role-based access, and verify the end-to-end process by creating and releasing a test document.
You must have completed Exercise 3 (Edit Workflow Templates) before starting this exercise. The Plan Development Workflow must be checked in and available.

content.title: Use Advanced Life Cycle Templates
content.objective: Convert a basic life cycle to advanced, associate it with a workflow template, configure access control, and validate the complete process.

steps.action: Navigate to Life Cycle Template Administration and edit the Plan Development Life Cycle.
steps.detail: The Life Cycle Template Administration utility lets you modify existing life cycle templates. You will convert this life cycle from basic to advanced so it can use workflow templates.
steps.hint: Access through Utilities > Life Cycle Template Administration.

steps.action: Change the life cycle type from Basic to Advanced.
steps.detail: Advanced life cycles support workflow association, meaning state transitions can be driven by workflow processes rather than manual Set State actions. This is required for enforcing formal review and approval processes.
steps.hint: Look for a Type dropdown or setting in the life cycle properties.

steps.action: In the Creation state Transitions tab, clear the Promote to Released transition and the Lock to Under Review transition.
steps.detail: These manual transitions are being replaced by workflow-driven transitions. Removing them ensures that state changes can only occur through the workflow process, providing full traceability.

steps.action: In the Workflow tab of the Creation state, browse for the Phase Process and select the Plan Development Workflow.
steps.detail: Associating the workflow with the Creation state means that when an object enters this state, the Plan Development Workflow will automatically start. The workflow then controls all subsequent state transitions.
steps.hint: Click Browse next to Phase Process and select the workflow from the list.

steps.action: Verify that no other states have phase or gate processes configured.
steps.detail: For this life cycle, only the Creation state should have an associated workflow. Other states should remain without workflow associations to keep the process simple.

tasks.title: Provide Read Access for Organization Members

steps.action: Click the Released state and navigate to the Roles tab.
steps.detail: The Roles tab defines which roles are active in a given life cycle state. Adding a Viewer role to the Released state allows you to grant read access to organization members for released documents.

steps.action: Add a Viewer role and add the PTC Publications organization as a participant.
steps.detail: Adding the entire organization as a participant means all members of PTC Publications will be assigned the Viewer role when a document reaches the Released state.
steps.hint: Click Add Role, select Viewer, then add the organization as a participant.

steps.action: In the Access Control tab, check the Read permission for the Viewer role.
steps.detail: This grants read-only access to organization members for released documents. They can view the document but cannot edit, delete, or change its state.

steps.action: Save and close the life cycle editor, then check in the life cycle template.
steps.detail: Checking in makes the updated life cycle available for use. New objects assigned this life cycle will now follow the advanced workflow-driven process.

tasks.title: Create Test Plan Document and Release It

steps.action: Create a new Plan document with the name 'Test Plan Document Validation'.
steps.detail: This document will use the Plan Development Life Cycle you just configured. Upon creation, the Plan Development Workflow should start automatically.
steps.hint: Create the document in the PTC Publications context where the life cycle is assigned.

steps.action: Complete the Submit Document task that appears in your task list.
steps.detail: The workflow's first activity (Submit Document) should appear in your Tasks table. Completing this task triggers the next step in the workflow — the Review Document activity.

steps.action: Complete the Review Document task by selecting the Release routing event.
steps.detail: Choosing Release sends the document through the approval path. The Set State robot will automatically transition the document to the Released state.
steps.hint: Select Release from the routing options on the task form.

steps.action: Verify that the document has reached the Released state.
steps.detail: Check the document's properties or life cycle history to confirm it is now in the Released state. This validates that the workflow and life cycle are working together correctly.

steps.action: Sign in as dgriffin/ptc in an incognito browser window.
steps.detail: Signing in as a different user (who is a member of the PTC Publications organization but not an administrator) lets you verify that the Viewer role and Read permission are working correctly.
steps.hint: Use a private/incognito browser window to avoid session conflicts.

steps.action: Search for 'Test Plan Document Validation' and verify it is visible but with limited access.
steps.detail: The document should appear in search results (confirming Read access), but the user should not be able to edit or change its state. This confirms the Viewer role is granting read-only access as intended.
steps.hint: Try to edit the document — you should receive an access denied message.

keyTakeaways: Converting a life cycle from basic to advanced enables workflow-driven state transitions with full traceability
Removing manual transitions ensures state changes can only occur through the workflow process
Role-based access control in life cycle states allows granting specific permissions (like Read) to organization members
Testing with a different user account validates that access control is working as intended
